title: Development of an intelligent design and simulation aid system for heat treatment processes based on LLM

agents.description: Qwen2.5:32b enhanced with RAG method for heat treatment knowledge Q&A and process recommendation. It retrieves relevant documents from the vector knowledge base and generates professional responses to user queries about heat treatment concepts, material properties, and process recommendations.

ChatGLM3-6b model used as an information extraction tool to extract process parameters and material data from natural language descriptions generated by Qwen2.5:32b-HT, and convert them into formatted POS and DAT files required by the COSMAP finite element analysis software.

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: The system uses a linear multi-agent pipeline. First, Qwen2.5:32b-HT handles knowledge Q&A and process recommendation using RAG. Once a process is confirmed by the user, ChatGLM3-6b extracts the process parameters from the natural language output and generates formatted POS and DAT files for COSMAP simulation software. The two agents operate sequentially with human confirmation between stages.

multi_agent_coordination_strategy.multi_agent_coordination_strategy_descri: Two agents operate in a linear sequential pipeline: Qwen2.5:32b-HT first performs knowledge retrieval and process recommendation, then ChatGLM3-6b extracts and converts the recommended process into structured files for COSMAP. Human confirmation acts as a handoff trigger between the recommendation and file generation stages.

human_integration.human_integration_description: Users interact with Chat-IMSHT through conversational dialogue to ask heat treatment knowledge questions and specify process goals. After the system recommends a process, the user confirms it before the system proceeds to generate COSMAP-compatible files. Users then manually load the generated POS, DAT, and part model files into COSMAP for simulation validation.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Intelligent design and simulation assistance for steel heat treatment processes, including knowledge Q&A, process recommendation, and automated generation of finite element analysis input files for COSMAP simulation software.
retrieval.problem_description: Designing heat treatment processes for steel is a complex multi-physics coupled problem. Current process design relies heavily on expert experience and costly experimentation. Training practitioners is expensive, and operating finite element simulation software like COSMAP requires significant expertise. There is no automated full-process system that integrates knowledge transfer, process recommendation, and simulation validation.
retrieval.proposed_solution: Chat-IMSHT, an intelligent heat treatment process design and simulation assistant system based on LLMs. It uses Qwen2.5:32b enhanced with RAG (Qwen2.5:32b-HT) for knowledge Q&A and process recommendation from a vector knowledge base containing over 1 million tokens of heat treatment knowledge. ChatGLM3-6b converts recommended processes from natural language into formatted POS and DAT files for COSMAP. A SHTKU evaluation framework with 221 multiple-choice questions across 7 categories assesses the system's heat treatment knowledge mastery.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: The study conducts controlled experiments to evaluate the system: (1) SHTKU benchmark testing comparing 10 LLMs on 221 heat treatment knowledge questions across 7 categories; (2) Process recommendation experiments using 27 process groups for a 20CrMnTi steel gear with three-fold cross-validation, where 70% of processes are used as the knowledge base and 30% as test targets; (3) Validation of generated POS/DAT files by running COSMAP simulations and comparing results against target parameters.

decision_quality_improvement: Qwen2.5:32b-HT achieved 94.54% accuracy on SHTKU, outperforming all other tested models including the base Qwen2.5:32b (93.67%) and human experts (78.40%).
RAG enhancement improved gpt-3.5-turbo by 26.24%, Llama3:8b by 5.89%, and Qwen2.5:32b by 0.87% on SHTKU.
Process recommendation MSE for hardness: 0.3068 (first set), 0.0208 (second set), 0.0338 (third set); MSE for carburization depth: 0.0056, 0.0049, 0.0022 respectively.
Closest hardness error in first test set was only 0.0011 HV and closest depth error was only 0.0062 mm.
reliability_metrics: Maximum SHTKU accuracy of 94.54% achieved by Qwen2.5:32b-HT.
Three-fold cross-validation performed on process recommendation task with 27 process groups.

ce_technique_effectiveness.retrieval-augmented-generation: RAG improved domain knowledge accuracy across all tested models; gpt-3.5-turbo improved by 26.24%, Llama3:8b by 5.89%, Qwen2.5:32b by 0.87%, demonstrating RAG's capability to enhance domain knowledge in LLMs.
ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Few-shot prompting with retrieved knowledge units enabled more accurate and professional heat treatment Q&A and process recommendations.

failure_modes: The language model sometimes recommends identical processes for different target parameters due to insufficient understanding of the intrinsic relationship between carburization process parameters and results when based on a small number of samples.
The system directly recommends results from the knowledge base rather than interpolating or extrapolating new process parameters.
limitations: Process recommendation relies on similarity matching within the knowledge base and may not generalize well beyond available samples.
The model does not adequately understand the intrinsic relationship between carburization process parameters and results with limited training samples.
The approach is difficult to quickly transfer to more materials without rebuilding the knowledge base.
The system currently focuses on gear parts (20CrMnTi steel) and may require adaptation for other part geometries or materials.

baseline_methods: Expertise Human (five undergraduates educated in heat treatment-related disciplines)
identified_research_gaps: The language model does not adequately understand the intrinsic relationship between the carburization process and the results based on a small number of samples, and directly recommends a result from the knowledge base.
Leveraging AI-Agent technology to utilize the interactive capabilities of LLMs to control equipment for automated heat treatment experiments.
Transferability of the approach to more materials beyond the current experimental setup.
